lines.line: # Introduction to the Oral History Interview

> Kenneth Heupel introduces himself as a history student from the College of Idaho, conducting an oral history interview with Janice Ogawa. The interview takes place on April 16th, 2024, at the Coldwell Banker Tomlinson offices in Meridian, Idaho. The purpose of the interview is to document Ogawa's experiences as a Japanese American woman in the Treasure Valley and her mother's experiences in incarceration camps during World War II. The project is a collaboration with the Idaho State Historical Society and supported by the NIH spotlight on the Humanities Grant program.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Oral History Interview
metadata.synopsis: Kenneth Heupel introduces himself as a history student from the College of Idaho, conducting an oral history interview with Janice Ogawa. The interview takes place on April 16th, 2024, at the Coldwell Banker Tomlinson offices in Meridian, Idaho. The purpose of the interview is to document Ogawa's experiences as a Japanese American woman in the Treasure Valley and her mother's experiences in incarceration camps during World War II. The project is a collaboration with the Idaho State Historical Society and supported by the NIH spotlight on the Humanities Grant program.

lines.line: > The speaker was born in Nampa, Idaho, and grew up on a farm with her parents and siblings. Her family was involved in farming, raising crops like sugar beets and potatoes, and later transitioned to owning a pool hall. The speaker reflects on the hardworking nature of her family and the values instilled by farming. She also touches on the social activities of the Japanese American Citizens League during her childhood, which was more social than political at the time.

metadata.title: Family Background and Farming Life
metadata.synopsis: The speaker was born in Nampa, Idaho, and grew up on a farm with her parents and siblings. Her family was involved in farming, raising crops like sugar beets and potatoes, and later transitioned to owning a pool hall. The speaker reflects on the hardworking nature of her family and the values instilled by farming. She also touches on the social activities of the Japanese American Citizens League during her childhood, which was more social than political at the time.

lines.line: # Mother's Experience in WWII Incarceration Camps

> The speaker's mother and her family were incarcerated in three different camps during World War II, including Pinedale, Tule Lake, and Minidoka. The speaker shares limited details about her mother's experiences, such as teaching second grade at Minidoka and the lack of privacy in the camps. Her mother's family lost everything when they were forced to move, and the speaker regrets not learning more about her mother's experiences before her memories faded.

metadata.title: Mother's Experience in WWII Incarceration Camps
metadata.synopsis: The speaker's mother and her family were incarcerated in three different camps during World War II, including Pinedale, Tule Lake, and Minidoka. The speaker shares limited details about her mother's experiences, such as teaching second grade at Minidoka and the lack of privacy in the camps. Her mother's family lost everything when they were forced to move, and the speaker regrets not learning more about her mother's experiences before her memories faded.

lines.line: # Family Military Service During and After WWII

> The speaker discusses the military service of her father's family, with several uncles serving in the U.S. military during and after World War II. Despite the internment of Japanese Americans, many from her father's family served to prove their allegiance to the U.S. The speaker notes the varied experiences of her uncles, from being denied service due to being Japanese to receiving land grants for their service. She also mentions the existence of a national Japanese American military unit, the 442nd Regimental Combat Team, which was highly decorated.

metadata.title: Family Military Service During and After WWII
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the military service of her father's family, with several uncles serving in the U.S. military during and after World War II. Despite the internment of Japanese Americans, many from her father's family served to prove their allegiance to the U.S. The speaker notes the varied experiences of her uncles, from being denied service due to being Japanese to receiving land grants for their service. She also mentions the existence of a national Japanese American military unit, the 442nd Regimental Combat Team, which was highly decorated.

lines.line: # Japanese American Community and JACL Activities

> The speaker describes the Japanese American community in Boise as growing, with many new members from out of state. She is actively involved in the Boise Valley Japanese American Citizens League (JACL), organizing social events that reflect Japanese culture and heritage. The JACL also engages in political advocacy, such as opposing the Lava Ridge Wind Project near Minidoka. The speaker emphasizes the importance of education and community in her work with the JACL and Friends of Minidoka.

metadata.title: Japanese American Community and JACL Activities
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes the Japanese American community in Boise as growing, with many new members from out of state. She is actively involved in the Boise Valley Japanese American Citizens League (JACL), organizing social events that reflect Japanese culture and heritage. The JACL also engages in political advocacy, such as opposing the Lava Ridge Wind Project near Minidoka. The speaker emphasizes the importance of education and community in her work with the JACL and Friends of Minidoka.

lines.line: # Career in Real Estate and Observations of Growth

> The speaker has had a long career in real estate, following in her father's footsteps. She has witnessed significant changes in the Treasure Valley, including rapid growth and increased property values. The speaker notes the influx of people from out of state and the cyclical nature of the real estate market. She expresses amazement at the technological advancements and the development of the area, which has brought both opportunities and challenges.

metadata.title: Career in Real Estate and Observations of Growth
metadata.synopsis: The speaker has had a long career in real estate, following in her father's footsteps. She has witnessed significant changes in the Treasure Valley, including rapid growth and increased property values. The speaker notes the influx of people from out of state and the cyclical nature of the real estate market. She expresses amazement at the technological advancements and the development of the area, which has brought both opportunities and challenges.

lines.line: > The speaker expresses pride in being from Idaho and plans to remain in the Treasure Valley. She feels welcomed and safe throughout the area and enjoys the sense of community. The speaker also shares her passion for playing pool, a hobby she picked up from her family's pool hall, and her upcoming induction into a national pool association's Hall of Fame. She reflects on the importance of preserving Japanese American culture and cuisine, although she notes that some traditions are fading with newer generations.

metadata.title: Personal Reflections and Idahoan Identity
metadata.synopsis: The speaker expresses pride in being from Idaho and plans to remain in the Treasure Valley. She feels welcomed and safe throughout the area and enjoys the sense of community. The speaker also shares her passion for playing pool, a hobby she picked up from her family's pool hall, and her upcoming induction into a national pool association's Hall of Fame. She reflects on the importance of preserving Japanese American culture and cuisine, although she notes that some traditions are fading with newer generations.

> Kenneth Heupel introduces himself as a history student from the College of Idaho, conducting an oral history interview with Janice Ogawa. The interview takes place on April 16th, 2024, at the Coldwell Banker Tomlinson offices in Meridian, Idaho. The purpose of the interview is to document Ogawa's experiences as a Japanese American woman in the Treasure Valley and her mother's experiences in incarceration camps during World War II. The project is a collaboration with the Idaho State Historical Society and supported by the NIH spotlight on the Humanities Grant program.

> The speaker was born in Nampa, Idaho, and grew up on a farm with her parents and siblings. Her family was involved in farming, raising crops like sugar beets and potatoes, and later transitioned to owning a pool hall. The speaker reflects on the hardworking nature of her family and the values instilled by farming. She also touches on the social activities of the Japanese American Citizens League during her childhood, which was more social than political at the time.
# Mother's Experience in WWII Incarceration Camps

> The speaker's mother and her family were incarcerated in three different camps during World War II, including Pinedale, Tule Lake, and Minidoka. The speaker shares limited details about her mother's experiences, such as teaching second grade at Minidoka and the lack of privacy in the camps. Her mother's family lost everything when they were forced to move, and the speaker regrets not learning more about her mother's experiences before her memories faded.
# Family Military Service During and After WWII

> The speaker discusses the military service of her father's family, with several uncles serving in the U.S. military during and after World War II. Despite the internment of Japanese Americans, many from her father's family served to prove their allegiance to the U.S. The speaker notes the varied experiences of her uncles, from being denied service due to being Japanese to receiving land grants for their service. She also mentions the existence of a national Japanese American military unit, the 442nd Regimental Combat Team, which was highly decorated.
# Japanese American Community and JACL Activities

> The speaker describes the Japanese American community in Boise as growing, with many new members from out of state. She is actively involved in the Boise Valley Japanese American Citizens League (JACL), organizing social events that reflect Japanese culture and heritage. She reflects on the importance of preserving Japanese American culture and cuisine, although she notes that some traditions are fading with newer generations.

story.description: The interview with Janis Ogawa, a Japanese American woman in Idaho, explores her family's history, particularly during World War II with a focus on her mother’s internment in camps like Minidoka. Janis recounts her upbringing on a farm, her family's transition to a pool hall business, and her personal and familial drive toward higher education. The discussion touches on the broader Japanese American community in Idaho, touching on assimilation, traditions like Japanese cuisine, and activities within the Boise Valley Japanese American Citizens League. Finally, the conversation addresses her career in real estate and her passion for pool, highlighting her induction into a national hall of fame as an amateur player.
